Location_name: Bideford - The Last Witches of England
Literature:
The Witches of Warboys (various)
Witch Hunt (various accounts)
Cultural_Significance: On 25 August 1682, Temperance Lloyd, Susanna Edwards and Mary Trembles were hanged at Heavitree Gallows in Exeter after being convicted of witchcraft in Bideford. They were the last people to be executed for witchcraft in England. The accusations began in Bideford where the women lived, and their trial drew enormous public attention. Their story remains one of the most significant and tragic episodes in English legal history.
Distinctive_Feature: A historic port town on the River Torridge where the witchcraft accusations originated. A plaque in the town commemorates the three women. The Bideford courthouse where they were first examined still stands.

Literature:
The Hound of the Baskervilles (Arthur Conan Doyle, 1902)
Prisoner of War (various accounts)
Cultural_Significance: Dartmoor Prison opened in 1809 to house thousands of French Napoleonic prisoners of war, followed by American prisoners from the War of 1812. Conditions were brutal — prisoners were so hungry they reportedly ate rats, grass and earthworms. On 6 April 1815 British guards fired on American prisoners in what became known as the Dartmoor Massacre, killing seven and wounding many more. The prison later held some of Britain's most dangerous criminals including Frank Mitchell, the Mad Axeman, who escaped and was later murdered by the Kray Brothers.
Distinctive_Feature: A forbidding granite prison on the open moorland of Dartmoor, still operational today. The Dartmoor Prison Museum houses manacles, straightjackets, escape stories and weapons made by prisoners across two centuries of incarceration.

Cultural_Significance: Heavitree Gallows was Devon's principal place of execution for centuries, located on the eastern edge of Exeter. Hundreds were hanged here including murderers, thieves and highway robbers. Women convicted of poisoning their husbands were burned at the stake. In 1682 the Bideford Witches — the last people executed for witchcraft in England — were hanged here. The last execution at Heavitree was Joan Stone in 1794, a 17-year-old girl hanged for setting fire to her master's house. Executions then moved to the newly built Exeter Prison.
Distinctive_Feature: A residential area of Exeter where the gallows once stood. Gallows Cross marks the historic execution site. The area's grim history is largely unmarked but the street names and local records preserve the memory of its dark past.

Location_name: Exeter Prison - The Man They Could Not Hang
Literature:
The Man They Could Not Hang (various accounts)
Babbacombe Lee (Fairport Convention, 1971)
Cultural_Significance: On 23 February 1885, John 'Babbacombe' Lee stood on the gallows at Exeter Prison three times. Three times the trapdoor failed to open. His execution was halted and his death sentence commuted to life imprisonment. Lee had been convicted of the murder of his employer Emma Keyse at Babbacombe, Torquay. He served 22 years and was released in 1907. His story became the subject of a Fairport Convention concept album and remains one of the strangest and most haunting episodes in British criminal history.
Distinctive_Feature: Exeter Prison stands on New North Road in the city centre, a forbidding Victorian jail still in operation today. The execution chamber where the gallows failed three times is no longer accessible but the building itself looms over the street.

Location_name: Babbacombe - Scene of the Unsolved Murder
Cultural_Significance: On the night of 14 November 1884, Miss Emma Keyse was found murdered at The Glen, her cottage at Babbacombe beach near Torquay. Her servant John Lee was convicted and sentenced to hang — but the gallows failed three times. Whether Lee was truly guilty has never been established with certainty. The case remains one of Devon's most debated Victorian crimes. Babbacombe beach itself is unchanged — a small, sheltered cove reached by a cliff railway, its peaceful appearance belying its notorious history.
Distinctive_Feature: A beautiful sheltered beach reached by the famous Babbacombe Cliff Railway. The atmosphere of the Victorian seaside resort is largely intact. The site of The Glen cottage where the murder took place is nearby.
